record.title: Viola White Cameron, 1974-07-25, Interview - MP3 Recording
record.subject: Latah County -- Idaho
record.occupation: Homemaker; store clerk; logging camp flunkey
record.keywords: forest fires schools strikes rodeos families children automobiles electricity lumberjacks logging camps chores food

point.title: Beal's Butte fire; Slabtown home;
point.synopsis: 1914 Beal's Butte fire. Father upset by loss of wood supply. Loss of Slabtown home in fire. The children went barefooted to save shoes, if they had them; they put shoes on in fire. Family went to Potlatch. Rebuilding after fire; help from town. Many lived in tents. Most of the homes were shanties.

point.title: School at Slabtown;
point.synopsis: School at Slabtown. Girls avoided mean teacher at Bovill by putting themselves back a grade. Miss Crawford asked it they liked other teachers as well as her. Walking to Bovill. Riding in a buggy, they noticed the full moon. Mother caught salmon in river by Slabtown, though people wouldn't believe her.

point.title: Little rodeos on Sunday afternoons;
point.synopsis: Bovill was "big city" when they moved in. Little rodeos on Sunday afternoons at Slabtown. Indian camping in Bovill meadows. Taught to be afraid of gypsies; mother hid brothers.

point.title: Baseball games; Local dances;
point.synopsis: The T.P.Jones' treated the children well. They gave rides in their fancy car. Baseball games. Local dances. Electric light plant and lights out. Chatauqua and circus. Revivals - people came forward to "Just As I Am". Bovill crowded with lumberjacks on Saturday night; children were always well treated. Parents' fear for children in strike; men needed the strike. (continued)

point.title: Hoboes; Outside agitators during strike;
point.synopsis: Hoboes. Outside agitators during strike. Packing in Marble Creek country. Feeling stupid because she didn't know the name for "hames." Evacuation in fire. Flunkeying in the camp. Men always kept the same place at table; they never spoke at table. Respect of jacks for women; walking them across dark meadow. Men liked food until they got used to it; then they went to Spokane.

point.title: Eleven children in family; Children's chores;
point.synopsis: Eleven children in family. Children's chores. Hot lunches everyday at home. Bread box was a lard container from camp. Doing dishes. They saw little of father because of his long work; mother rarely had time to go anywhere except to help a sick neighbor. Their first work out was picking strawberries - they failed to make their board and brought home the itch.

point.title: Subsequent work in Bovill and Spokane; Parker's store in Bovill;
point.synopsis: Subsequent work in Bovill and Spokane. Parker's store in Bovill.

point.title: Pat Malone;
point.synopsis: Pat Malone was part of everybody's family. His love of children of all ages. Teasing Pat.

record.description: and Grace Ryan (sister) Flunkeying and lumberjacks. Social life of Bovill. Mother's work, Loss of Slabtown house in 1914 fire. 7-25-24 1 hr LS
record.transcript: Download the PDF Index No transcript available.
record.rights: Digital format copyright: University of Idaho Library. Original Copyright: Latah County Historical Society
